content.text:
```json
{
  "signature": "The Thema of K.331 is a compact 18-bar structure in A major built around a beautifully balanced two-part design: an A section (bars 1–8) that moves from an imperfect close at bar 4 to a full perfect cadence at bar 8, and a B section (bars 9–18) that ventures briefly but pointedly away from home. The harmonic surprise arrives at bar 13, where Mozart tonicizes E major — the key a fifth above — treating the shared chord as both the tonic of A major and the dominant of E, before a quiet diatonic return to A major at bar 17 closes the theme with a final perfect cadence at bar 18. Harmonic momentum is gently managed throughout: the B section lingers on A major harmony through bars 9–11 before a half-cadence at bar 12 creates just enough suspense to make the E major detour feel earned rather than abrupt.",
  "significance": "This careful choreography of tension and release — staying close to home, stepping away just once, and returning with quiet certainty — gives the theme its feeling of natural inevitability, the musical equivalent of a thought that completes itself exactly as expected, yet somehow still feels satisfying every time it arrives.",
  "connection": "Bach's Fugue in A major from the Well-Tempered Clavier Book I (BWV 864) shares with this Mozart theme both its key and a Classical instinct for tonal economy — the gravitational pull between A major and its dominant, E major, is equally central to Bach's harmonic world. Where Mozart achieves that pull through a single, elegant tonicization, Bach characteristically weaves the same tonal relationship into the very architecture of his subject and answer, so that the tension between the two keys operates across the entire piece rather than a handful of bars. Both composers seem to understand A major as a key whose brightness invites restraint: the expressive weight comes not from dramatic excursions but from how precisely the music knows when to leave home and when to return."
}
```
